name: Platform-Neutral Access and Procurement Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Public sector defines platform-neutral access requirements and initiates a risk assessment for alternative authentication paths, reducing reliance on iOS/Android trust signals. Systemic: Vendor-dependency index falls by 25%, and fallback-readiness tests show a 40% improvement in cross-platform continuity drills. Strategic: Denmark gains procurement leverage and policy credibility to scale platform-neutrality across AltID and future digital identity ecosystems.
options: Adopt a platform-neutral baseline that mandates WebAuthn/OpenID Connect-based authenticators and policy-backed procurement clauses.
Pilot a two-track path combining AltID compatibility testing and a formal fallback protocol with vendor-neutral signal attestation.
Launch a decentralized SSI-based stack with verifiable credentials and a government-backed token-based funding model to fund interoperability.
review: Controls Procurement Velocity vs. Platform-Neutral Resilience. Weakness: The options fail to consider long-term governance and funding stability across EU integration.

name: Fallback Authentication Demonstrator Acceleration Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Establish two non-production demonstrators—mobile-oriented on privacy-respecting OS and a hardware-token/browser fallback—to evaluate standards-based authentication against a simulated environment. Systemic: Demonstrations cover 90% of defined test scenarios and reduce mean time to recover from simulated outages by 30%. Strategic: Builds credibility to influence procurement standards and AltID-related design language.
options: Launch dual non-production demonstrators with WebAuthn/OpenID Connect on privacy-respecting platforms and a hardware-token fallback to assess cross-platform interoperability.
Secure formal commitments with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and MitID operator to validate non-production fallback paths and publish risk-mitigation plans.
Prototype a decentralized SSI stack with verifiable credentials, GrapheneOS-compatible attestations, and a government-backed token model to fund interoperability.
review: Controls Speed vs. Real-World Feasibility. Weakness: The options fail to consider how demonstrators translate into binding procurement actions or interoperable EU standards.

name: Standards-Pressure and EU Alignment Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Engage with EU/NSIS/ETSI bodies to embed platform-neutral fallback requirements into national and EU standards and publish a gap analysis against AltID. Systemic: European interoperability index improves by 20%, and consented fallback criteria become embedded in 2–3 major procurement frameworks within 18 months. Strategic: Denmark secures a seat in EU wallet standardization and reduces vendor lock-in risk across member states.
options: Engage EU standard bodies to codify platform-neutral fallback requirements into national and EU procurement standards.
Collaborate with EU-Digital Identity Wallet Working Groups to harmonize signals and interoperability tests across member states.
Establish a cross-border, open-source identity stack governance initiative funded by a government-backed grant model and token-based incentives.
review: Controls Compliance Burden vs. Interoperability. Weakness: The options may understate regulatory friction and sovereignty concerns that slow EU-wide standard adoption.

name: Coalition-Building and Public-Trust Governance Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Establish a formal, publicly visible coalition including privacy advocates, civil society, academics, security experts, and policymakers; publish a governance charter. Systemic: Public-trust index improves by 15%, and coalition breadth grows by 40% across phase transitions, increasing likelihood of procurement approvals. Strategic: Legitimizes platform-neutral push and reduces policy-blocking risk, enabling sustained advocacy for fallback capabilities.
options: Form a formal multi-stakeholder advisory group with privacy advocates and civil society to guide policy and procurement.
Publish a resilience-focused public report with transparent timelines and decision gates to enhance legitimacy.
Establish a citizen-owned governance layer backed by distributed ledger and smart contracts to fund and oversee fallback infrastructure.
review: Controls Trust vs. Expediency. Weakness: The options fail to address how governance bodies will sustain funding and accountability if political attention wanes.

name: Innovative Funding and Economic Resilience Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Reallocate funding across the three phases with milestone-based disbursements and strict go/no-go gates. Systemic: Budget variance remains within ±5% across phases, reducing financial risk while preserving flexibility for necessary experimentation. Strategic: Ensures ongoing political support, maintaining cadence for platform-neutral investments and credible fallback readiness.
options: Reallocate funding across the three phases with milestone-based disbursements and strict go/no-go gates.
Seek EU co-funding and private-sector contributions to finance demonstration work and standardization efforts.
Create a government-backed sovereign identity fund leveraging a token-based incentive model to underwrite platform-neutral infrastructure and open-source contributions.
review: Controls Budget Rigidity vs. Experimental Flexibility. Weakness: The options fail to specify credible exit clauses if funding becomes constrained or delayed.

name: Resilience-Driven Identity Governance Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Public sector signs a platform-neutral governance charter and initiates continuity drills across identity touchpoints. Systemic: vendor-dependency index falls by 20%, and fallback-readiness drills improve by 35%. Strategic: Denmark gains durable resilience and a governance framework enabling platform-neutral procurement, but at higher administrative overhead; Trade-off: resilience and continuity vs. slower decision cycles and governance burden.
options: Adopt a platform-neutral governance charter with centralized oversight, baseline fallback requirements, and phased procurement controls.
Create a cross-agency resilience cell to manage procurement standards, continuity planning, and independent audits while piloting AltID-compatible fallback tests.
Deploy a blockchain-enabled governance layer with open-source attestation protocols and a token-based incentive system to crowdsource interoperability decisions.
review: Controls Resilience vs. Agility. Weakness: The options fail to consider how rapid crisis-response coordination would be maintained under heavier governance overhead.

name: Open Identity Fabric Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Public sector publishes open APIs and baseline standards for cross-vendor authentication signals. Systemic: cross-vendor interoperability index improves by 25%, reducing cross-vendor integration costs by 15%. Strategic: Denmark achieves durable interoperability with reduced lock-in risk, but governance and maintenance of an open fabric introduce ongoing costs; Trade-off: interoperability expansion vs. governance overhead.
options: Publish open APIs and baseline standards for cross-vendor authentication signals to enable plug-and-play interoperability across platforms.
Develop and pilot reference implementations for an open identity fabric with vendor-neutral attestations and AltID compatibility.
Launch a federated DID-based identity fabric with verifiable credentials, edge nodes, and a government-backed token economy to incentivize open-source collaboration.
review: Controls Interoperability vs. Governance Overhead. Weakness: The options fail to consider resource constraints for open-source governance and long-term maintenance costs.

name: Regulatory Signals and Procurement Resilience Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Policy adopts flexible procurement frameworks and initiates a regulatory sandbox. Systemic: Time-to-approval for platform-neutral solutions decreases by 30%; supplier competition increases by 15%. Strategic: Denmark gains leverage in EU policy and reduces lock-in risk, but regulatory complexity may slow adoption; Trade-off: agility vs. standardization.
options: Institute strict, EU-aligned procurement clauses that require vendor-neutral fallback evaluation.
Pilot conditional procurement linking continuity criteria to vendor-agnostic fallback readiness, with staged gates.
Institute an EU-wide regulatory sandbox with token-based incentives to develop platform-neutral fallback modules and AI-driven compliance checks.
review: Controls Compliance Burden vs. Interoperability. Weakness: The options fail to address cross-border legal risk and EU procurement politics.

name: Citizen-Centric Engagement and Transparency Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Public trust metrics improve; participation in engagements increases. Systemic: engagement participation rises by 25%, and policy acceptance improves by 15%. Strategic: Strengthens legitimacy of platform-neutral efforts, but elevates governance overhead; Trade-off: public input quality and reach vs. timely decision-making.
options: Publish a public roadmap with clear milestones, decision gates, and quarterly updates to maintain transparency.
Formalize multi-stakeholder consultations with privacy advocates, academics, and civil society to review drafts and standards.
Implement a tokenized, blockchain-backed citizen budget and decision-making platform to govern fallback investments and procurement choices.
review: Controls Public Trust vs. Governance Overhead. Weakness: The options do not specify safeguards against capture by special interests or manipulation of engagement processes.

name: Security Architecture Diversity Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Security posture improves; systemic: mean time to detect breaches reduces by 35% and annual security costs rise by 12% due to diversification. Strategic: Increases resilience and reduces lock-in risk, but adds integration complexity and cost; Trade-off: breadth of security controls vs. management overhead.
options: Adopt diverse hardware-backed and software-based authenticators with vendor-neutral attestations.
Pilot cross-signature verification with multiple attestation providers and independent audits across components.
Implement cryptographic vault with post-quantum security, multi-cloud/multi-provider attestation networks, and an orchestration layer.
review: Controls Security Breadth vs. Integration Complexity. Weakness: The options lack explicit budget constraints and the risk of fragmentation across services.

name: Cross-Platform Identity Ecosystem Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Public sector defines cross-platform authentication interoperability requirements and a formal fallback protocol. Systemic: cross-vendor integration readiness index improves by 25% and procurement costs drop by 12% due to modular contracts. Strategic: Denmark gains durable platform-neutral capability and stronger alignment with EU wallet initiatives.
options: Publish a cross-platform interoperability baseline with WebAuthn/OpenID Connect and require vendor-neutral attestation in procurement.
Develop a two-track pilot applying AltID compatibility testing and vendor-neutral attestations to demonstrate cross-vendor trust anchors.
Create a platform-neutral, decentralized identity fabric leveraging verifiable credentials and a government-backed token economy to fund interoperability.
review: Controls Platform Lock-in vs. Procurement Speed. Weakness: The options fail to consider long-term governance and funding stability across EU integration.

name: Procurement-Driven Continuity Assurance Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Procurement rules include continuity-based gating and defined multi-vendor exit options. Systemic: vendor-lock-in index falls by 18%, and fallback-readiness testing success reaches 75%. Strategic: Governance and procurement gain credibility to drive platform-neutral implementations, but administrative overhead increases.
options: Institute continuity-focused procurement clauses that require multi-vendor attestations and fallback testing with defined exit options.
Establish conditional funding gates tied to procurement flexibility and vendor-neutral signal verification.
Launch a government-backed platform-neutral procurement framework coupled with a living contract model using smart legal agreements and ongoing external security reviews.
review: Controls Continuity vs. Agility. Weakness: The options fail to consider how rapid crisis-response coordination would be maintained under heavier governance overhead.

name: Interoperability Funding and EU Alignment Financing Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Open funding channels for cross-border tests. Systemic: EU-aligned funding share increases by 30%, enabling multi-country pilots and reducing cross-border project costs by 15%. Strategic: Denmark secures EU alignment and reduces vendor lock-in risk across member states.
options: Pursue national funding with staged milestones and clear cost-sharing rules.
Create a cross-border grant consortium with EU partners and private sector co-funding to support fallback demonstrations.
Launch a European Platform-Neutral Identity Fund financed by a mixed public-private instrument and token-based incentives for open-source contributions.
review: Controls Funding Access vs. Sovereign Autonomy. Weakness: The options may not consider domestic economic constraints and capacity to absorb cross-border pilots.

name: Civil Society Oversight and Accountability Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Public accountability charter and transparency mechanisms are published. Systemic: civil-society participation increases by 30%, and governance overhead increases by 20%. Strategic: Public trust and legitimacy improve, enabling smoother policy adoption despite higher overhead.
options: Publish a public accountability charter and quarterly transparency reports.
Establish a formal multi-stakeholder oversight board with privacy and procurement experts.
Create a citizen-owned digital identity oversight DAO with token incentives and parliamentary auditing powers.
review: Controls Public Trust vs. Governance Burden. Weakness: The options do not specify safeguards against capture by vested interests.

name: Diverse Cryptographic Ecosystem Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Security posture expands with diversified cryptographic controls. Systemic: algorithm agility index improves by 40%, and annual security costs rise by 12% due to diversification. Strategic: Reduces long-term lock-in risk and fosters innovation, but integration complexity increases.
options: Adopt a multi-signer cryptographic framework with vendor-neutral attestations.
Pilot cross-provider attestation services and independent security reviews across components.
Implement post-quantum cryptography with a distributed cryptographic vault and cross-cloud attestations leveraging multiparty computation.
review: Controls Security Breadth vs. Integration Complexity. Weakness: The options lack explicit budget constraints and risk of fragmentation.
